lessons.title: Lead Scoring Fundamentals: From Gut Feel to Data
lessons.title_es: Fundamentos del lead scoring: del instinto a los datos
lessons.description: Replace subjective sales intuition with a repeatable, data-driven scoring model that ranks every lead by close probability.
lessons.description_es: Reemplaza la intuición subjetiva de ventas con un modelo de puntuación repetible y basado en datos que clasifica cada lead por probabilidad de cierre.
lessons.content:
Why Most Lead Scoring Systems Fail

The typical lead scoring setup assigns points for job title, company size, and email opens — then wonders why sales ignores the scores. The problem is that these inputs correlate with profile fit, not purchase intent. A VP at a 500-person company who opened your email twice is not more likely to buy than a Director at a 50-person company who visited your pricing page four times.

Effective lead scoring requires two distinct models working together: a fit score (how well the lead matches your ideal customer profile) and an intent score (how active they are in a buying motion right now). A lead needs both to be sales-ready.

Building the Fit Score

Fit score inputs come from firmographic and demographic data. Pull from your CRM enrichment tool (Clearbit, Apollo, or Clay) and score each dimension:

  • Industry match: Map to your 14 target niches. Exact match = 20 pts, adjacent = 10 pts, off-niche = 0 pts.
  • Company size: Define your sweet spot (e.g., 10–200 employees = 20 pts; 200–500 = 10 pts; 500+ or under 10 = 0 pts). Adjust based on your actual closed-won data.
  • Job title / seniority: Decision-maker titles = 20 pts; influencer titles = 10 pts; individual contributors = 0 pts. Build a list of exact title strings for each tier.
  • Geography: If you serve specific markets, US target markets = 15 pts, secondary markets = 5 pts, out-of-territory = 0 pts.
  • Technology signals: Tools like BuiltWith or Wappalyzer reveal tech stack. Using a competitor's tool = 10 pts (active in the market); using complementary tools = 15 pts (integration opportunity).

Building the Intent Score

Intent score inputs come from behavioral signals — what the lead does, not who they are:

  • Pricing page visit: 25 pts (strongest purchase signal available)
  • Demo request or contact form: 40 pts (hand-raise — this lead should be called within 4 hours)
  • Multiple blog article reads in one session: 15 pts
  • Email link click (to a product page): 10 pts
  • Return website visit within 7 days: 15 pts
  • Webinar attendance: 20 pts

Apply score decay: reduce intent score by 50% every 30 days of inactivity. A lead who visited your pricing page 6 months ago and has done nothing since is not in a buying motion today. Decay keeps your hot lead list current and prevents sales from chasing cold signals.

lessons.title: AI-Powered Scoring: Training and Deployment
lessons.title_es: Scoring impulsado por IA: entrenamiento y despliegue
lessons.description: Use your historical CRM data to train a predictive model that outperforms rule-based scoring.
lessons.description_es: Usa tus datos históricos de CRM para entrenar un modelo predictivo que supera el scoring basado en reglas.
lessons.content:
When Rule-Based Scoring Hits Its Ceiling

Rule-based scoring (the point system from Lesson 1) works well in the first 6–12 months and for teams under 500 leads/month. Beyond that volume, the interactions between signals become too complex for manual rules. A lead with a high fit score and moderate intent might close better than a high-intent / low-fit lead — but only for specific product tiers. Predictive AI scoring captures these non-linear interactions automatically.

The Data Requirements

You need a minimum of 200 closed-won and 200 closed-lost deals in your CRM before predictive scoring is statistically reliable. Each record needs:

  • Lead source and enriched firmographic fields (industry, size, title)
  • Behavioral data (email events, website visit log, form submissions)
  • Deal outcome (won/lost) and time-to-close
  • Deal value (for revenue-weighted scoring)

Export this as a CSV. Every row is a historical lead; every column is a feature; the target column is 1 (won) or 0 (lost). This is your training dataset.

Training with Claude API or a No-Code Tool

For teams without a data scientist, two routes work well:

  • No-code option: Upload your training CSV to Polymer or Obviously AI. These tools auto-identify feature importance, train a classification model, and return a score formula. Output the score formula (coefficients per feature) and implement it as calculated fields in your CRM.
  • Claude API option: Send your training data in structured batches to Claude with this system prompt: "You are a sales data analyst. Given historical lead records and their outcomes, identify the top 10 predictive features for closed-won deals and weight them 1–100 based on correlation strength." Use the returned weights to build a weighted scoring formula.

Either approach gives you a data-derived score formula superior to manually assigned point values. Rebuild the model every quarter as you accumulate new won/lost data — features that predicted success 12 months ago may not predict it today.

Implementing Score Thresholds and Routing

Define three routing tiers based on your combined fit + intent score:

  • Score 80–100 (Hot): Auto-assign to a senior AE, trigger immediate Slack alert, enroll in a 3-touch high-urgency sequence with a call on touch 1.
  • Score 50–79 (Warm): Assign to SDR queue, enroll in standard 8-touch nurture sequence, call on touch 3.
  • Score 0–49 (Cold): Marketing nurture only. No sales outreach until score improves to Warm tier.

Review routing decisions monthly. If Hot leads are converting below 20%, your threshold is too low. If you have fewer than 10 Hot leads per month per rep, it may be too high.

lessons.title: Integrating Scores into Your Sales Workflow
lessons.title_es: Integrando el scoring en tu flujo de trabajo de ventas
lessons.description: Wire lead scores directly into your CRM, sequences, and rep dashboards so they drive daily behavior.
lessons.description_es: Conecta las puntuaciones directamente en tu CRM, secuencias y paneles de representantes para que impulsen el comportamiento diario.
lessons.content:
Scores Only Work If Sales Sees Them

The most sophisticated scoring model fails if it lives in a spreadsheet that nobody opens. Lead scores must be a visible, first-class field in every view a sales rep uses daily. This lesson is about integration — making the score a behavioral driver, not a reporting artifact.

CRM Configuration

Set up three fields in your CRM for every contact record:

  • Fit Score (0–100): Updated via enrichment API webhook — static until firmographic data changes.
  • Intent Score (0–100): Updated in real-time via website tracking pixel events, email click events, and form submissions.
  • Combined Score (0–100): Calculated field. Recommended formula: (Fit Score × 0.4) + (Intent Score × 0.6). Bias toward intent because a perfect-fit lead who isn't buying right now is a future opportunity, not a current one.

Sort every lead queue view by Combined Score descending. The rep's first call of every day should always be their highest-scored lead.

Sequence Enrollment Triggers

Connect your scoring thresholds to automatic sequence enrollment:

  • Combined Score crosses 80 → enroll in "Hot Outreach" sequence, notify rep in Slack
  • Intent Score increases by 20+ points in 7 days → "Re-engaged Lead" alert to rep
  • Score decays below 40 → remove from sales queue, enroll in long-term nurture sequence

These automations run in your CRM workflow builder (HubSpot workflows, Salesforce Flow, or a Zapier/Make integration for lighter CRMs). The rep wakes up to a prioritized queue — they don't need to evaluate who to call. The system does that work.

Rep Dashboards and Weekly Scoring Reviews

Build a weekly "Score Movement" dashboard that shows each rep's leads sorted by score change over the past 7 days. Leads that jumped from Warm to Hot this week are the priority conversation starters for Monday morning. Leads that dropped from Warm to Cold are the "what changed?" conversation — sometimes a competitor win, sometimes a budget freeze you need to know about.

Hold a 15-minute weekly scoring review with your sales team. Show the score distribution, celebrate follow-through on Hot leads, and discuss any anomalies. Teams that review scores together close more leads from those reviews than teams who leave scoring as a background process.

lessons.title: Continuous Scoring Improvement
lessons.description: Build a feedback loop that makes your scoring model smarter every quarter.
lessons.description_es: Construye un ciclo de retroalimentación que haga tu modelo de scoring más inteligente cada trimestre.
lessons.content:
Scoring Is Never Finished

Lead scoring is not a one-time setup — it's a living model that must be recalibrated as your market, product, and ICP evolve. Teams that set scoring rules in Q1 and don't revisit them by Q3 are making decisions based on outdated signal weights. This lesson covers the audit and improvement cycle that keeps your scoring model accurate.

The Quarterly Scoring Audit

Run this audit every quarter using your closed-won and closed-lost data from the period:

  • Score accuracy check: Of all leads scored 80+, what percentage closed? This is your model precision. Target: 25%+ close rate from Hot leads. Below 15% means your Hot threshold is too permissive.
  • Coverage check: What percentage of closed-won deals were scored 50+ at the time of first outreach? This is your model recall. Below 60% means high-value deals are falling through your scoring net — you have a blind spot in your feature set.
  • Feature drift analysis: Pull the top 10 features by weight. Have any become saturated? (If 80% of your leads score maximum on a feature, it no longer differentiates.) Replace saturated features with new signals.

Adding New Signals Over Time

Your signal library should expand as your marketing stack grows. High-value signals to add as you instrument them:

  • Third-party intent data: Bombora, G2 Buyer Intent, or LinkedIn Sales Navigator Intent signals show when a company is actively researching solutions in your category — even before they visit your site. Add these as a 20-25 pt bonus on intent score.
  • Content engagement depth: Did they read to the bottom of your pricing page? Did they watch 80% of your demo video? Scroll depth and video watch time are stronger intent signals than a raw page visit.
  • Email reply sentiment: If your sequences generate replies, classify them (positive, neutral, objection, unsubscribe) and score each type. A reply — even an objection — is 5× more valuable than a silent email open.

The Closed-Lost Interview Loop

The most underused improvement signal is the closed-lost debrief. When a Hot lead (score 80+) closes lost, require the rep to log one of five reasons: wrong timing, budget, competitor, no decision, or qualification miss. After three months, pattern analysis of these tags reveals systematic scoring errors. If 40% of your Hot-lost deals cite "wrong timing," your intent model is detecting research-stage activity, not purchase-stage urgency — you need stronger late-stage intent signals.
